lesson_summary: This lesson builds a case for change in healthcare by moving from structural and economic pressures, to human consequences, to technological acceleration, consumer expectations, and finally the leadership gap physicians must help close. Most source slides are best preserved as keep-dense singletons because their instructional value comes from simultaneous contrast or synthesis. The technology slide is the primary cluster candidate due to its higher concept density and multiple distinct explanatory beats.

plan_units.learning_objective: Explain the structural forces reshaping physician practice, including rising national health expenditures and the shift from independent practice to health-system employment.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From isolated clinic thinking to enterprise-level awareness through paired economic and structural trend evidence
plan_units.rationale: Core opening problem frame from the source corpus. High pedagogical weight because it establishes why individual optimization is no longer sufficient. Keep-dense because the slide's value is the simultaneous whole: spending growth plus employment-structure shift together create the enterprise-navigation imperative.
plan_units.title: The Economic & Structural Reality

plan_units.learning_objective: Describe how administrative waste and bureaucratic burden contribute to physician burnout, and recognize burnout as a system design problem rather than an individual resilience failure.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From seeing inefficiency as financial waste to recognizing it as a human systems-design crisis through linking administrative bloat to burnout
plan_units.rationale: Central explanatory bridge from macro-system trends to clinician experience. Dense but best understood as a stark paired-statistic gestalt. Keep-dense because splitting would weaken the intended contrast between waste and human cost.
plan_units.title: The Human Cost: System Waste & Burnout

plan_units.learning_objective: Summarize how accelerating knowledge growth and emerging technologies such as AI, remote monitoring, and virtual reality are changing clinical practice and training.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From technology overwhelm to innovation stewardship through staged evidence about knowledge acceleration, AI adoption, and care-delivery tools
plan_units.rationale: High concept density with multiple explanatory beats: knowledge-doubling acceleration, AI adoption without training, remote monitoring impact, and VR training implications. Benefits from progressive disclosure.
plan_units.title: The Knowledge Explosion & New Technologies

plan_units.learning_objective: Identify the tension between rapid AI adoption in practice and limited formal training for safe clinical oversight.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From technology overwhelm to innovation stewardship through staged evidence about knowledge acceleration, AI adoption, and care-delivery tools
plan_units.rationale: Supports the head unit by isolating the main risk signal embedded in the broader technology trend. Necessary tension beat for the cluster.
plan_units.title: Technology Tension: Adoption Is Outpacing Oversight

plan_units.learning_objective: Give concrete examples of how digital health and VR can improve outcomes and training when implemented thoughtfully.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From technology overwhelm to innovation stewardship through staged evidence about knowledge acceleration, AI adoption, and care-delivery tools
plan_units.rationale: Concrete exemplar beat using SMART remote monitoring and VR procedural rehearsal to show upside, not just disruption.
plan_units.title: Technology in Practice: From Reactive Care to Safer Learning

plan_units.learning_objective: Explain how changing patient expectations are driving organizations to design more seamless, digitally enabled access journeys.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From passive patient assumptions to consumer-centered system design through the digital front door journey
plan_units.rationale: Important market and experience trend, but conceptually coherent as a single journey illustration. Keep-dense because the patient journey metaphor depends on seeing the end-to-end path together.
plan_units.title: The Consumer Shift & The Digital Front Door

plan_units.learning_objective: Recognize the gap between physicians' interest in leadership and the limited availability of formal executive, business, and systems-thinking training.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From desire for influence to recognition of a capability gap through contrasting physician leadership interest with limited formal training
plan_units.rationale: Capstone transition to the course purpose. High pedagogical weight because it justifies the learner's role. Keep-dense because the headline contrast between demand for leadership and lack of training is the main instructional value.
plan_units.title: The Leadership Gap

plan_units.learning_objective: Synthesize the major trends shaping modern healthcare and conclude why deliberate physician leadership is necessary to translate complexity and technology into better care and clinician well-being.
plan_units.narrative_arc: From fragmented trend awareness to a coherent case for change through synthesis of structural, human, technological, consumer, and leadership pressures
plan_units.rationale: Explicit summary and concluding statement from the source corpus. Keep-dense because synthesis is the point; the value lies in holding the system trends together at once.
plan_units.title: Part 2 Summary & Knowledge Check
